label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, so I need to figure out if an 8x8 chessboard with two diagonally opposite corners removed can be tiled perfectly with 31 dominoes. Each domino covers two adjacent squares. Let me think about this step by step. First, I remember that a standard chessboard has 64 squares. If we remove two corners that are diagonally opposite, that leaves us with 62 squares. Since each domino covers two squares, 31 dominoes would cover exactly 62 squares. So the total number of squares is even, which is a good start. But just having an even number isn't enough, right? There must be some other condition. I recall something about coloring the chessboard. Normally, a chessboard has alternating black and white squares. Each domino, when placed on the board, will always cover one black and one white square, regardless of its orientation. So, if the entire board has an equal number of black and white squares, then domino tiling is possible. But if there's an imbalance, then it might not be. Let me check that. Let's say the original chessboard has 32 black and 32 white squares. When we remove two diagonally opposite corners, what happens? Well, diagonally opposite corners on a chessboard are the same color. For example, if the top-left corner is black, then the bottom-right corner is also black. Similarly, the other two corners (top-right and bottom-left) are white. So removing two corners of the same color would leave us with 30 squares of that color and 32 of the other. Therefore, after removing two corners, the remaining squares have 30 and 32 squares of each color. But each domino must cover one black and one white square. If there are 31 dominoes, they would need to cover 31 black and 31 white squares. However, we have an imbalance of two squares (30 vs. 32). That means there's no way to cover all squares with dominoes because there's a mismatch in the number of black and white squares. Wait, let me confirm that. Suppose we remove two black squares. Then there are 30 black and 32 white. Each domino must cover one black and one white. If we use 30 dominoes to cover all black squares, that would cover 30 white squares, leaving 2 white squares uncovered. But those two squares can't be adjacent, so you can't place another domino. Alternatively, trying to place 31 dominoes would require 31 black and 31 white squares, but there are only 30 black squares. So either way, it's impossible. Therefore, the answer should be no. But let me think again if I missed something. Is there a possibility that even with the color imbalance, some tiling might still be possible?
